description: Figure 4 presents the tensile mechanical properties of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to different heat treatment processes. Panel (a) displays the engineering stress‒strain curves, while panel (b) shows the corresponding true stress‒true strain curves (solid lines) and strain hardening rate curves (dashed lines) as a function of true strain. Four conditions are compared: multi-stage heat treatment (MHT, black), single-stage treatment at 1000°C (S1000, red), 1050°C (S1050, blue), and 1100°C (S1100, cyan). The curves demonstrate that all specimens achieve high yield strength and ultimate tensile strength with excellent ductility at 25°C. Specifically, S1000 exhibits YS of ~955 MPa and UTS of ~1126 MPa with 55.5% total elongation, while S1050 shows comparable strength (~945 MPa YS, ~1113 MPa UTS) and ductility (~55.8% TEL). The MHT specimen maintains high strength (~910 MPa YS, ~1076 MPa UTS) and good ductility (~50.9% TEL) despite having a larger effective grain size (7.31 μm) compared to S1050 (3.84 μm), whereas S1100 shows reduced strength (~827 MPa YS, ~998 MPa UTS). The strain hardening rate curves in (b) exhibit three distinct stages (I, II, III), with maximum hardening rates approaching ~2000 MPa at true strains of 0.20‒0.22. These results indicate that grain size variations within this range do not significantly compromise the strength-ductility balance, supporting the effectiveness of the multi-stage heat treatment strategy for optimizing grain boundary characteristics while maintaining excellent tensile properties.
